author: misilva73
content:
Two thoughts on this:

- What "unbounded" buys an attacker is modest. The block-level state-gas cap already bounds the worst case at one block's worth of state. Whether that's consumed by one tx or several doesn't change the disk work the network has to do (unlike regular-gas, where a single long-running tx is meaningfully worse than many short ones). The cap on state-gas would mostly impact blocking building, not validation. 
- A 2x cap (~33.5M) would break the main use case the reservoir model exists for. A max-size 24 KiB contract needs ~37.6M state-gas just for code deposit (24,576 × 1530), plus ~183.6k for the new account and a ~5M constructor budget, which adds up to around 43M total. 2x doesn't cover that.

We could derive a cap from the deployment worst case (e.g., `MAX_DEPLOYED_CODE_SIZE × CPSB + intrinsic + constructor_budget`), but I don't think the added complexity is worth it — it's another parameter to justify and maintain for a fairly small marginal benefit over the block-level cap.

author: misilva73
content: To pick up on the call refill clarification and the call data floor cap update, I created this [PR](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/11706). Would be great to get <@755913785620824175> and client's feedback, specially as the call data floor make lead to implementation and test changes

author: misilva73
content: I just reran the analysis and now Erigon is performing on par with all the other clients. This means that based on the current numbers, we don't need to change any prices to these opcodes 🎉
